shortTitle: Pov: You're a Spill Responder at Every Rank

content.text: You mop up a coffee spill in the break room your first week. Three paper towels, minor defeat.
content.imageDescription: A new employee wearing standard work clothes kneels in a break room, cleaning a spilled cup of coffee with three scrunched paper towels. The tiled floor reflects the mess, with a half-empty cup lying nearby. The scene is framed from above to show the full extent of the spill and the worker's frustrated, focused expression.

content.text: The next day, it’s a leaky bottle of floor cleaner in the janitor’s closet. Your gloves melt at the edges; someone laughs.
content.imageDescription: Inside a cramped janitor’s closet cluttered with supplies, the worker struggles to contain a leak from a tipped bottle of floor cleaner that spreads a puddle toward their feet. Their rubber gloves show visible melting at the fingertips. In the doorway, a coworker is seen laughing, partially obstructed as if peeking in.

content.text: By Friday, it’s paint thinner under the stairs. You start reading the warning labels.
content.imageDescription: Under a metal stairwell, the worker crouches beside a new puddle of spilled paint thinner, reading the warning label on the can with a worried expression. Various caution signs and scattered cleaning tools are visible around them.

content.text: At rank 4, you’re called for a mystery puddle in the chemistry lab that smells like burning rubber and dread. By 6, it’s an unknown substance in a parking lot, bubbling, slowly dissolving someone’s tire. You want to go back to coffee.
content.imageDescription: In a science lab with beakers and safety signs, the worker inspects a mysterious puddle shimmering on the tile, holding their nose as fumes rise. Then, in an outdoor parking lot, they stare at a bubbling chemical spill as it eats through a parked car’s tire, their body posture showing apprehension and longing for simpler tasks.

content.text: Level 8, you suit up. The spill glows under fluorescents. You wonder if they’ll name a syndrome after you.
content.imageDescription: In a locker room, the worker dons a full hazmat suit. In the next shot, they approach a glowing, unnatural stain spreading across the lab floor under harsh fluorescent lights, their face obscured by a visor. Their body language suggests anxious curiosity.

content.text: Level 9: alarms, evacuation, incident command. Sirens, blinking lights. Nobody makes eye contact. Level 10 is a drum overturned in the loading bay, smoking, evacuating half the block. Your badge says “Spill Responder.” It should say “Last Line.” You stand in your suit, watching foam hiss and spread across the floor. Nobody remembers the coffee spill. You do.
content.imageDescription: A sequence showing the worker in their suit during a facility emergency: alarms flash red, staff evacuate past incident command with equipment, and sirens glow outside. Next, an overturned chemical drum emits smoke in a loading bay, emergency vehicles outside as foam spreads underfoot. The worker stands apart, looking at their badge marked 'Spill Responder,' lost in thought amid the chaos.

content.text: Every level contains all the others. You clean, suit up, walk in. You go home, soap scalding hot, and dream of safe, dry floors. You chose this, you remind yourself, every time your boots stick to something you can’t pronounce. That wraps it up for today. Tell me—what’s the scariest mess you ever cleaned? See you in the next one.
content.imageDescription: At home, the worker scrubs their hands under steaming water at a kitchen sink, eyes tired and reflective. A dreamlike overlay shows clean, spotless floors. In the final shot, their boots rest on the doormat, glistening with a mysterious residue. The scene pulls back as if inviting the viewer to respond, signaling the end of the story.
